us-iran-ceasefire-mou-signed-june-17-hor.headline: US-Iran ceasefire MOU signed June 17; Hormuz re-closed June 20 after Israeli Lebanon strikes; US Navy announced 'widened route' June 27 — strait status remains contested
us-iran-ceasefire-mou-signed-june-17-hor.summary: A 60-day ceasefire MOU was signed June 17 requiring Iran to allow free Hormuz transit, but Iran re-closed the strait on June 20 citing Israeli attacks in Lebanon; on June 27 the US Navy's JMIC announced a widened passage near Oman as a de-facto challenge to Iranian control, leaving the strait's status ambiguous and oil markets volatile (WTI near $81, down from a ~$120 wartime high).
us-iran-ceasefire-mou-signed-june-17-hor.target_indicator: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
us-iran-ceasefire-mou-signed-june-17-hor.rationale: The Strait of Hormuz is the single most material chokepoint in the tracker. Iran re-closed the strait within 72 hours of the June 17 MOU on the pretext of Israeli Lebanon strikes; the US Navy's June 27 'widened route' announcement is an unprecedented direct challenge to Iranian sovereignty claims and signals active brinkmanship. The status should be coded 1 (contested) moving toward 2 — this is not a resolved situation. With ~20–27% of seaborne oil transiting the strait, any re-escalation reverts WTI toward wartime highs and transmits immediately into CPI, EM debt, and food/fertilizer (s18) indicators. The WTI indicator should be updated to ~$81 (down from $120 wartime high, but fragile).

boj-raises-policy-rate-to-1-0-at-june-16.headline: BOJ raises policy rate to 1.0% at June 16 MPM; hawkish board members call for hikes every 'few months' toward 2% neutral; 10-year JGB yield at ~2.61–2.65%
boj-raises-policy-rate-to-1-0-at-june-16.summary: The Bank of Japan lifted its policy rate to 1.0% on June 16 and simultaneously cut its JGB purchase plan to ~¥200bn/month. BOJ Deputy Governor Himino and board member Tamura both signaled further hikes every few months targeting a ~2% neutral rate, with Tamura warning that if upside inflation risks intensify the BOJ should 'accelerate the pace of rate hikes without hesitation.'
boj-raises-policy-rate-to-1-0-at-june-16.rationale: The June 16 BOJ rate hike to 1% — combined with accelerated QT (JGB purchases halved to ¥200bn/month) and explicit board rhetoric toward a 2% neutral — is a step-change for the carry trade and JGB market. The 10-year JGB is at 2.61–2.65%, a multi-decade high that, combined with Japan's fiscal arithmetic, pressures carry-trade unwinds and USD/JPY volatility. The 30-year JGB yield indicator and Takaichi plan financing clarity indicators both need updating. The July 31 MPM is the next critical watch date.

china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.headline: China blacklists MP Materials and USA Rare Earth on June 22 — targeting the two US government-backed domestic producers with categorical dual-use export bans
china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.summary: On June 22, China's MOFCOM placed 10 US entities on its export control list, with MP Materials (Mountain Pass mine) and USA Rare Earth (Texas mine-to-magnet) being the strategically significant targets; the action bars any entity worldwide from transferring Chinese-origin dual-use goods to these firms — a tighter 'categorical prohibition' vs. the prior license-based regime. Simultaneously, China's Finance Ministry barred ~46 US firms from Chinese government procurement.

proposed_indicator.label: US domestic RE producer blacklist status (0 none / 1 entity-specific ban / 2 sector-wide ban)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = no US rare-earth producers on China's export control entity list; 1 = named entity-specific bans in place (current: MP Materials + USA Rare Earth blacklisted, barring Chinese dual-use equipment/tech transfers globally); 2 = China escalates to sector-wide prohibition covering all US-linked rare-earth processors or imposes production quota cuts targeting Western supply chains. Sources: MOFCOM entity list updates, company SEC filings, USGS supply-chain reports.

china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.rationale: The existing s6 indicators track export license flow and the Nov-2025 pause expiry, but do not capture the new 'categorical entity designation' instrument that China is now deploying. Blacklisting the US government's two flagship domestic rare-earth producers directly attacks the strategic diversification effort — cutting off their access to Chinese processing equipment, IP, and tech transfers — and signals willingness to escalate to sector-wide controls. This new instrument warrants a dedicated indicator, especially with the November 10 pause window approaching and neodymium-praseodymium oxide prices up ~6x since January 2026.

genius-act-aml-sanctions-rulemaking-adva.headline: GENIUS Act AML/sanctions rulemaking advanced by Treasury/FinCEN (April 9) ahead of July 18 regulatory deadline; stablecoin supply and T-bill demand implications crystallizing
genius-act-aml-sanctions-rulemaking-adva.summary: Treasury's FinCEN and OFAC issued a joint proposed rule on April 9 to implement GENIUS Act AML and sanctions compliance requirements for permitted payment stablecoin issuers, with all implementing regulations due by July 18, 2026 (one year post-enactment). The 1:1 T-bill/cash reserve mandate means stablecoin supply growth directly drives marginal Treasury demand — a structural new buyer class for short-dated debt.
genius-act-aml-sanctions-rulemaking-adva.target_indicator: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)
genius-act-aml-sanctions-rulemaking-adva.rationale: With the July 18 regulatory deadline imminent and the proposed AML rule still in comment period, implementation friction is rising toward '1 gaps' — not because of political opposition but because the 120-day licensing window, state-vs-federal framework reconciliation, and Big Tech exclusion rules all create compliance uncertainty. More importantly, the Treasury-demand vector (stablecoins mandated to hold T-bills 1:1) is now a live market structural factor as total USD stablecoin supply is growing; this indicator needs monitoring for both friction and the T-bill demand boost that could affect s1 (basis trade) and s12 (de-dollarization) dynamics.

bofa-estimates-ai-hyperscaler-capex-appr.headline: BofA estimates AI hyperscaler CapEx approaching $800bn in 2026 (+67% YoY), trending toward $1 trillion in 2027; capex-vs-revenue gap concern intensifying
bofa-estimates-ai-hyperscaler-capex-appr.summary: BofA Global Research estimates hyperscaler capital expenditures could approach $800 billion in 2026, a 67% increase from 2025, trending toward $1 trillion in 2027; investor scrutiny over whether economic returns will justify this level of investment and how quickly spending translates into sustained earnings growth remains the key risk, with Amundi flagging AI capex as the main equity support even as macro uncertainty rises.
bofa-estimates-ai-hyperscaler-capex-appr.target_indicator: AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern (0 closing / 1 wide / 2 widening fast)
bofa-estimates-ai-hyperscaler-capex-appr.rationale: An $800bn capex run-rate (+67% YoY) with investor uncertainty explicitly flagged by major sell-side and buy-side houses moves the capex-vs-revenue gap indicator firmly to '2 — widening fast.' The scale now implies massive debt issuance by hyperscalers (relevant to US IG OAS in s8) and grid/power demand acceleration (relevant to s15_ai_power). At ~$1 trillion in 2027 projections, this is no longer a sector risk but a macro balance-sheet risk — especially if the Middle East shock raises cost of capital while revenue ramp lags.

eba-june-2026-risk-assessment-eu-eea-hea.headline: EBA June 2026 Risk Assessment: EU/EEA headline inflation rising to 3.0–3.1% on energy shock; EA GDP slowing to 0.9%; bank-NBFI linkages up 10.4% YoY — energy-driven credit risk spreading beyond directly exposed sectors
eba-june-2026-risk-assessment-eu-eea-hea.summary: The EBA's June 2026 Risk Assessment Report finds euro area inflation rising from 2.1% to 3.0% (EU: 2.5% to 3.1%) driven by Middle East energy prices, EA GDP growth slowing to just 0.9%, and EU bank exposures to non-bank financial institutions (NBFIs) rising 10.4% YoY — creating a compounding channel where energy inflation, tighter monetary conditions, and bank-NBFI linkages could trigger broader asset-quality deterioration beyond directly energy-exposed portfolios.

proposed_story.name: European energy-stagflation & bank-NBFI contagion channel
proposed_story.why: The Middle East conflict has created a persistent energy-price shock that is simultaneously driving euro area inflation back above 3%, compressing EA GDP to near-stagnation (0.9%), forcing the ECB into a hawkish pause, and widening EU bank exposures to NBFIs by over 10% YoY. This creates a novel compounding risk loop not captured by the existing s10_france story (which focuses on OAT-Bund spreads and political stability) nor s20_sea_lanes (which tracks Hormuz status and WTI). The EBA explicitly flags that second-order credit-quality deterioration — spreading beyond directly energy-exposed sectors via tighter financial conditions and bank-NBFI linkages — is the key systemic threat to EU banking in 2H 2026.

indicators.label: Euro area HICP energy component YoY (%)
indicators.rubric: Tracks the energy sub-index of euro area HICP inflation (Eurostat monthly release). Values >10% signal acute pass-through risk to core inflation and ECB hawkish pivot; 5–10% = elevated; <5% = contained. Current: materially elevated driven by post-Hormuz oil/gas prices.

indicators.label: EU bank-to-NBFI exposure growth YoY (%)
indicators.rubric: Tracks YoY growth in EU/EEA bank exposures to non-bank financial institutions (OFC category in EBA FINREP data). >10% = rising linkage risk (current: +10.4%); >20% = systemic concern; <5% = stable. A rapid build-up amplifies contagion in a stress scenario where NBFI assets (private credit, leveraged loans) come under pressure.

indicators.label: EA GDP growth vs ECB rate path divergence (0 aligned / 1 diverging / 2 stagflation trap)
indicators.rubric: 0 = ECB easing in line with slowing growth (soft landing); 1 = ECB on hold or hiking while EA GDP <1.5% (current: EA at 0.9%, ECB hawkish pause); 2 = EA GDP contracting while ECB holds/hikes due to persistent energy inflation — stagflation scenario with acute credit deterioration risk across household and corporate portfolios.

eba-june-2026-risk-assessment-eu-eea-hea.rationale: The EBA's June 2026 RAR is a primary supervisory source, not a market opinion piece. It documents a materializing feedback loop: Middle East energy shock → euro area inflation reacceleration → ECB hawkish pause → compressed growth → widening bank-NBFI linkages → latent credit deterioration risk across EU bank portfolios. This is structurally distinct from s10_france (political fragility) and s20_sea_lanes (Hormuz status/WTI): it is a second-order financial-stability channel specific to EU/EEA banking. With EA GDP at 0.9% and Euribor rates moving upward simultaneously, the risk of a EU-specific stagflation credit event in 2H 2026 is material and currently untracked.

us-iran-ceasefire-collapses-for-4th-cons.headline: US–Iran ceasefire collapses for 4th consecutive day; Iran attacks Bahrain & Kuwait, Hormuz status contested
us-iran-ceasefire-collapses-for-4th-cons.summary: June 26–29 2026: the US and Iran exchanged fresh strikes near the Strait of Hormuz after Iran attacked Bahrain and Kuwait following new US air strikes. A Trump administration official confirmed a 'stand down for now' Sunday evening, but Hormuz traffic remains under IRGC pressure; oil futures sit near $69–72/bbl after spiking to $114 in March.
us-iran-ceasefire-collapses-for-4th-cons.target_indicator: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
us-iran-ceasefire-collapses-for-4th-cons.rationale: The Hormuz status indicator is the primary read-point for s20_sea_lanes. Current situation: ceasefire violently broken on June 26–29, Iran strikes Bahrain and Kuwait, US retaliates, Hormuz traffic partially re-disrupted after IRGC claims sovereignty over waterway management under the June 17 MOU. The indicator should be marked '1 contested' (not full closure, but tanker convoys require naval escort and Iran is demanding toll/control rights post-60-day window). WTI ~$69, Brent ~$72 — still materially elevated vs pre-war ~$55 — confirms partial resolution but persistent risk premium. This is the highest-materiality single development of the week.

boj-officials-signal-rate-hikes-every-fe.headline: BOJ officials signal rate hikes every few months; 30yr JGB yields at 3.88%, 10yr at 2.61% — carry trade unwind risk acute
boj-officials-signal-rate-hikes-every-fe.summary: BOJ Governor Ueda reaffirmed rate-hike commitment on June 25; board member Tamura called for hikes 'every few months' targeting a neutral rate near 2%. Japan 30yr JGB yield sits at 3.88% (up ~97bps YoY) and 10yr at ~2.61%, with USD/JPY around 159–160 — yen weakening alongside rising domestic yields is an atypical and destabilizing combination.
boj-officials-signal-rate-hikes-every-fe.rationale: The 30yr JGB yield at 3.88% — up nearly 100bps year-on-year — is a direct hit to the s2_japan story's 30yr JGB indicator. StoneX notes markets appear 'unwilling to allow both the yen and the long end of the JGB curve to remain simultaneously protected,' and BofA has revised its USD/JPY year-end forecast lower to 152. Combined yen weakness (USD/JPY ~159) and surging super-long yields signal mounting fiscal sustainability concern and growing carry-unwind tail risk. The July 31 BOJ meeting is a live catalyst. The 'Takaichi plan financing clarity' sub-indicator should remain at '1 unclear' given the simultaneous yen weakness and fiscal spending pressure.

china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.headline: China blacklists MP Materials and USA Rare Earth — first entity-specific ban on US domestic RE producers effective June 22
china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.summary: China's MOFCOM on June 22, 2026 added 10 US entities to its export control list, including MP Materials (Mountain Pass mine, CA) and USA Rare Earth (Texas mine-to-magnet project), prohibiting Chinese firms from supplying dual-use items to them with immediate effect. The action was framed as retaliation for the Pentagon adding ~80 Chinese firms to its military-companies list in early June.
china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.target_indicator: US domestic RE producer blacklist (0 none / 1 entity-specific ban / 2 sector-wide ban)
china-blacklists-mp-materials-and-usa-ra.rationale: This is a direct, precise hit on the 'US domestic RE producer blacklist' indicator in s6_rare_earths. The measure moves the indicator from '0 none' to '1 entity-specific ban'. The ban targets the two largest US domestic producers—MP Materials (Mountain Pass) and USA Rare Earth—which are central to US government-backed supply-chain diversification. Benchmark Minerals confirms the ban is entity-specific rather than blanket, but analysts at Discovery Alert warn it establishes precedent for escalation to sector-wide. NdPr oxide prices have already surged sixfold between January–June 2026 per other data, further validating the stress. The Nov-2025 pause remains nominally in place until Nov 2026 but this action effectively creates parallel, direct suppression of US domestic capacity.

hyperscaler-ai-capex-approaches-800bn-in.headline: Hyperscaler AI capex approaches $800bn in 2026 (+67% YoY), trending toward $1 trillion in 2027 — revenue gap concern widens
hyperscaler-ai-capex-approaches-800bn-in.summary: BofA Global Research (June 15 CIO letter) estimates hyperscaler capex could reach $800 billion in 2026, a 67% increase from 2025, and trend toward $1 trillion in 2027. Investor scrutiny remains high over whether economic returns will justify the spending, with broad AI infrastructure debt and concentration risk elevated.
hyperscaler-ai-capex-approaches-800bn-in.target_indicator: AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern (0 closing / 1 wide / 2 widening fast)
hyperscaler-ai-capex-approaches-800bn-in.rationale: BofA's estimate of $800bn in hyperscaler capex for 2026 (+67% YoY) directly updates the s8_ai_capex story. The gap concern indicator should be marked '2 widening fast': capex is scaling geometrically while revenue is not matching pace. The BofA CIO letter itself flags investor scrutiny over 'whether the economic return will ultimately justify this level of investment.' The BlackRock macro outlook similarly notes that 'the AI-enthralled market narrative' echoes prior euphoria cycles. The scale of debt issuance underpinning these data-center builds is the systemic link to the 'GPU-backed debt refinancing stress' and US IG OAS proxies in the same story.

iran-claims-sovereign-control-over-hormu.headline: Iran claims sovereign control over Hormuz maritime management post-MOU — structural chokepoint sovereignty risk now embedded in ceasefire terms
iran-claims-sovereign-control-over-hormu.summary: The June 17 US–Iran MOU allows Hormuz transit 'with no charge for 60 days only,' after which Iran and Oman will determine 'future administration and maritime services.' Iran's IRGC also redefined Hormuz as a 'vast operational area' extending from Jask to Siri Island in May 2026. Multiple analysts warn Iran has achieved de-facto leverage over global oil flow regardless of the final deal.

proposed_indicator.label: Hormuz sovereignty/toll risk (0 free passage / 1 contested management / 2 Iran toll/control enforced)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = Hormuz operates under international freedom-of-navigation norms with no state claiming administrative control; 1 = MOU or ceasefire terms grant Iran/Oman co-management rights, transit operationally possible but under negotiated terms with 60-day sunset clauses and recurring re-escalation risk; 2 = Iran actively enforcing tolls, restricting allied nation shipping, or formally asserting sovereign control recognized in international frameworks. Current reading: 1. Review trigger: expiry of 60-day MOU window (~mid-August 2026) or new IRGC interdiction incident.

iran-claims-sovereign-control-over-hormu.rationale: The existing s20_sea_lanes story tracks whether Hormuz is 'open/contested/closed' on an operational basis, but the June 17 MOU introduces a structurally new risk dimension: Iran has extracted a clause giving it co-management and future toll rights over the world's most critical oil chokepoint. This is not captured by the binary open/closed indicator. Even when Hormuz is operationally transitable, embedded toll-and-sovereignty provisions create a recurring extortion risk for oil importers (Japan, India, South Korea, China) and a potential WTO/UNCLOS challenge. A separate indicator for this structural shift is warranted to distinguish between 'ships are moving' (operational) and 'Iran has legitimized control' (strategic/structural).

genius-act-implementation-rulemaking-und.headline: GENIUS Act implementation rulemaking underway — OCC/Treasury/FinCEN NPRMs active, 18-month compliance clock running since July 2025
genius-act-implementation-rulemaking-und.summary: The GENIUS Act (signed July 18, 2025) is now in multi-agency implementation: OCC issued proposed rules on stablecoin issuance requirements, while Treasury/FinCEN/OFAC issued joint AML/sanctions NPRMs. The effective date is the earlier of January 2027 or 120 days after final rules are issued, but rulemaking complexity and interagency coordination gaps could cause friction ahead of the deadline.
genius-act-implementation-rulemaking-und.target_indicator: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)
genius-act-implementation-rulemaking-und.rationale: The GENIUS Act is law (July 2025) but the OCC, FinCEN, and OFAC are still in proposed-rulemaking phase as of mid-2026, with the compliance clock running toward a January 2027 hard deadline. The OCC bulletin confirms that the majority of rules are proposed but not finalized; FinCEN/OFAC AML/sanctions rules were issued separately in April 2026, still in comment period. This multi-agency divergence in rulemaking timelines — and the challenge of coordinating federal vs. state supervisory regimes under §10 billion thresholds — places this indicator at '1 gaps' rather than '0 smooth'. The risk is that issuers face conflicting or delayed final guidance, delaying the stablecoin Treasury-demand dynamic the tracker monitors. Current total USD stablecoin supply is growing rapidly (28%+ YoY per WEF data), making regulatory clarity increasingly urgent.
